pattern: Placing the tallest people first means every k value becomes an absolute position, because no one placed later can ever count as 'in front and taller' for someone already seated -- so a straightforward sorted insertion reconstructs the whole queue.
hints: If you only ever look at people from tallest to shortest, then whenever you place someone, everyone already placed is at least as tall as them -- so their k value tells you exactly how many of the already-placed people must come before them.
Sort people by height descending, and for ties, by k ascending; then insert each person into a growing result list at the index equal to their own k.

Because of the sort order, a person's k is always within the bounds of the list built so far, so inserting at index k (shifting everyone from that point on to the right) is always valid and never needs to look past the current length.

edge_inputs.description: same height entries given out of k order in the input
all distinct heights already front-loaded by tallest

stress.description: a random valid arrangement is generated first, then k values are derived from it so the input is always reconstructable
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    heights = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    order = heights[:]
    rng.shuffle(order)
    people = []
    for i in range(n):
        h = order[i]
        k = sum(1 for j in range(i) if order[j] >= h)
        people.append([h, k])
    rng.shuffle(people)
    return [people]
stress.note: k values always come from a real valid arrangement, so a reconstruction is guaranteed to exist

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case for the insertions
